one_line_takeaway: A candid single-author systems paper arguing that local-first, zero-LLM memory plus Bayesian per-agent trust scoring defends against ASI06 memory poisoning, with a genuinely honest ablation showing only the adaptive re-ranker (not the graph/pattern layers) improves ranking -- but the security claim rests on architecture and synthetic/n=1 evaluation rather than adversarial red-teaming.
key_finding: Search latency is 10.6ms median at 100 memories but degrades superlinearly (1.17s at 5,000) due to brute-force O(n^2) TF-IDF/graph work; concurrency is lock-error-free but throughput collapses from ~220 ops/sec at 2 writers to ~25 ops/sec (P95 754ms) at 10 writers under SQLite single-writer serialization. Trust scoring separates benign vs. malicious agents with a gap of 0.898 in the single-poisoner scenario (benign 0.946 vs malicious 0.048) and degrades a sleeper agent's trust by 72.4% (0.902 -> 0.249, gap 0.653) with zero false positives. The ablation is the paper's most honest result: FTS5, +TF-IDF, +Graph, and full-system configs ALL give identical MRR 0.90 / NDCG@5 0.441 -- the structural layers add no ranking value -- and only the adaptive re-ranker lifts NDCG@5 to 0.900 (+104%) and NDCG@10 to 0.728 (+56%) for +20ms, while leaving MRR unchanged.
core_contribution: SuperLocalMemory (SLM) is a single-author, local-first persistent-memory system for multi-agent AI whose central security claim is that keeping all memory on-device (a single SQLite file, no network, no LLM inference) eliminates the cloud-mediated attack surface behind OWASP ASI06 memory poisoning. On top of this architecture the paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a four-layer memory stack (SQLite+FTS5 storage, materialized-path hierarchy, Leiden-clustered TF-IDF knowledge graph, Beta-Binomial pattern learning); (2) a Bayesian per-agent trust-scoring framework with per-memory provenance that runs in enforcement mode (agents with trust < 0.3 are blocked from writes/deletes); and (3) a zero-LLM adaptive learning-to-rank layer that re-ranks retrieval using three-layer behavioral analysis. GraphRAG's Leiden clustering, MACLA's Beta-Binomial confidence model, and LambdaRank/LightGBM re-ranking are explicitly adapted from cited prior work, not claimed as novel; the novelty claim is the specific COMBINATION of fully-local zero-LLM operation with trust-based poisoning defense.
threat_model: Attacker is a malicious or compromised agent with write access to shared agent memory; OWASP ASI06 memory poisoning (persistent corruption that survives session boundaries and influences future decisions). The paper enumerates three vectors: (a) direct injection (false facts written to shared memory), (b) indirect injection (malicious external/tool/web content stored as trusted memory), and (c) gradual erosion / 'sleeper agent' (an agent builds trust via benign ops, then injects poison). Motivating real-world incidents cited (not the author's own): the Gemini memory exploit, calendar-invite poisoning (73% high-critical success over 14 scenarios), and Lakera's sleeper-agent injection. The claimed structural defense is that a local, single-user store removes multi-tenant exposure, network interception, opaque vendor-controlled provenance, and vendor lock-in; the active defense is Bayesian trust scoring plus provenance tracking.
limitations: The headline 'gap = 0.90' is the single-poisoner number (0.898); the harder sleeper-agent gap is only 0.653, a distinction the abstract blurs. The +104% NDCG gain is undercut by an author-acknowledged evaluation circularity: graded relevance labels are derived from the system's own importance scores, which the ranker consumes as one of its nine features -- so it is partly scored on predicting a signal it can see. Nearly all benchmarks use synthetic templated data with known topic labels; the only human validation is an n=1, self-conducted pilot (one developer, 182 memories, 70 judgments) with no formal user study. The system is explicitly NOT evaluated on standard memory benchmarks (LoCoMo shown as N/E in the comparison table while competitors report scores), so cross-system quality claims are not head-to-head. Trust scores are not yet used as a ranking feature. Graph construction is capped at 10,000 memories and O(n^2) without the (unevaluated) optional HNSW path. The 'poisoning defense' is largely architectural/assumed (local => no remote writes) rather than demonstrated against adaptive attackers who game the trust update, and the author notes AI writing tools were used for the manuscript.

datasets_used: Synthetic templated memories across 5 topic categories (web development, machine learning, database design, DevOps, API design) with known ground-truth topic labels
Adversarial trust scenarios: 10 agents x 200 operations per agent (benign baseline, single poisoner 9+1, sleeper normal-then-inject)
n=1 pilot: one developer's 182 organic memories, 20 queries over 5 project contexts, 70 human relevance judgments on a 0-3 scale
quantitative_results: Search latency: 10.6ms median at 100 memories; 124.3ms at 1,000; 1,172ms at 5,000 (superlinear O(n^2) growth)
Storage: 1.4KB per memory at 10,000 (13.6MB total), amortizing from 44KB at 100 memories
Concurrency: zero 'database is locked' errors; peak 220.8 ops/sec at 2 writers, dropping to 24.9 ops/sec / P95 754.2ms at 10 writers
Graph construction: 0.28s (100 mem) -> 10.6s (1,000) -> 277s (5,000); edges 935 -> 2.46M; Leiden finds 6-7 top-level communities, depth-3 subclustering
Trust single-poisoner: benign 0.946 vs malicious 0.048, gap 0.898, zero false positives
Layer ablation at 1,000 memories: FTS5 / +TF-IDF / +Graph / full-system all identical at MRR 0.90, NDCG@5 0.441, NDCG@10 0.466
Adaptive re-ranker: NDCG@5 0.441 -> 0.900 (+104%), NDCG@10 0.466 -> 0.728 (+56%), +20ms latency, MRR held at 0.90
Pilot (human-judged): MRR 0.70, NDCG@5 0.90; rating distribution 38.6% highly-relevant / 24.3% relevant / 28.6% slightly / 8.6% irrelevant
Integrates with 17+ tools via MCP; open-source under MIT
baselines_compared: Internal layer ablation: FTS5-only vs +TF-IDF reranking vs +Graph clusters vs full-system vs +Adaptive ranker (the primary controlled comparison)
Adversarial baseline: benign-only 10-agent scenario vs single-poisoner vs sleeper

Qualitative feature-matrix comparison in Table 5 (SLM vs Mem0, MemOS, A-MEM, Cognee, Zep, Letta) reporting others' published benchmark numbers (e.g., Mem0 LoCoMo J=66.9, Letta 74.0, Zep 72.3 LongMemEval) while SLM itself is N/E on LoCoMo -- i.e., NOT a head-to-head quality benchmark
